item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The top image looks very not great but apart from that, the writing is good.

item.title: Operational Staff Post
item.content:encoded:
Images replaced with compliant ones.

Book is under CC BY-SA 3.0 by Tom Murphy VII.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'd think the dude was some sort of parody or joke if I hadn't actually known people who were that petty.
Moronic too, considering it sounds like most of his complaints could be solved by building a fence for a tiny fraction of what he invested in revenge.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What happened to Jerry Hubert's copy of Volume VI? It says that agents discovered the manuscripts in his car after 2270-Alpha, and Jerry wrote in his e-mail "i just read the part about eternal consequences. thats not for real right?" so he presumably received Volume VI with his purchase, but you mentioned earlier on that the Foundation has been unable to locate Volume VI at time of writing.
As the 200th upvoter, I have to say that this is a truly epic fist.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The pettiness is believable (I dealt with my share of irate, single minded idiots as IT support), I'd upvote twice if I could.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love this skip.

The reason why? The dissonance between how solemn the description is; it's like many other SCPs about demonic entities and things relating to them. The story the description gives of a horrific entity and a terrible price to pay for those who are foolish enough to make deals with it.

All this leads up to… some random jerkoff using the power of a god of war to get revenge on his annoying neighbour. It's funny because it's a total bait-and-switch to what you would expect.

Upvoted. Upvoted hard.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So I came here from the boardgame scp and literally laughed out loud when I realized why the crosslink was called "fuck this guy in particular." This article has some fantastic buildup to an absolutely hilarious punchline.

However I have to admit that it doesn't hold up so we'll on the second read through. It's really hard to believe that some redneck moron (who judging by the emails is clearly struggling to understand many concepts written in the books and doesn't treat the subject matter seriously) was able to successfully complete the ridiculously complicated ritual when the Foundation apparently can't. It's harder to believe that he was the first and only known individual believed to have ever successfully summoned the entity which was worshipped for millennia. And I frankly cannot believe that the Foundation could possibly have covered up a colossal colorful arm reaching from the sun to the Earth and punching the surface with the force of a nuclear bomb.

So yeah, no vote for now. If you can find a way to keep the excellent joke but also fix those plotholes then I will gladly upvote it.

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Timer Expired. Edits made as discussed below. Author may revert at any time.

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team

+ Show Block
- Hide Block

To be honest there is not a whole lot of room in this article for linking to another SCP, at least from my eyes. Its particularly self-contained in terms of narrative, and I feel that getting a link there to another skip may be a little contrived. That said, we'll stick to inline links to Site-81 and the MC&D hub. As such, I am recommending the following edits:

Changing this:

Written or electronic information pertaining to SCP-2270 or the incantations relating to SCP-2270 (specifically instances of SCP-2270-A) is to be strictly controlled, and all original documents kept in a high security storage locker at Site-81. Under no circumstances are unauthorized personnel to access or view the aforementioned documents.

To this:

Written or electronic information pertaining to SCP-2270 or the incantations relating to SCP-2270 (specifically instances of SCP-2270-A) is to be strictly controlled, and all original documents kept in a high security storage locker at Site-81. Under no circumstances are unauthorized personnel to access or view the aforementioned documents.

Changing this:

After an initial investigation was unable to locate Mr. Hubert, a raid on his Augusta home discovered a series of emails to a contact from the Rutherford Group, a supplier for the American division of Marshall, Carter and Dark, Ltd. In the messages, Mr. Hubert requests information pertaining to SCP-2270, and later purchases the entirety of a nearly complete set of SCP-2270-A for $75,000.

To this:

After an initial investigation was unable to locate Mr. Hubert, a raid on his Augusta home discovered a series of emails to a contact from the Rutherford Group, a supplier for the American division of Marshall, Carter and Dark, Ltd. In the messages, Mr. Hubert requests information pertaining to SCP-2270, and later purchases the entirety of a nearly complete set of SCP-2270-A for $75,000.

If anyone has other/better examples that could be used here please leave a comment saying so!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Eh, I started glazing over the ritual stuff once it became apparent that it was just straight Holders-esque descriptions of exacting terribleness. The punchline wasn't enough to actually make it work as a story. -1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Good stuff. The pettiness is what makes it. Good use of images too. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I upvoted at the picture and that upvote stays after reading.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I rather suspect you have too much empathy to easily kill, very little willingness to engage in significant violence, and are able to rationally balance your impulses against serious consequences

Maybe I just like to fantasize about lazy assholes who use the handicapped parking spot getting smote by the fist of an angry and uncaring god while I drag myself across the parking lot because those assholes just had to take the spot that isn't theirs.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is really freaking awesome… Has anyone considered using it on 682? I know it won't work, but I wanna see how bad this fails.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
And, you know, if I could call down Nergal's wrath on people who use handicapped parking when they have no authorization or need to do so, but use it anyways just because they're lazy assholes… the consequences would probably be worth it.

You would use the equivalent yield of the entire US arsenal of strategic weapons to kill millions (and probably trigger WWIII; killing basically everybody) in order to kill one person for parking illegally?

I honestly doubt this, for one thing it isn't actually that hard to kill a person, so if you really were sociopathic, capable of interpersonal violence, and totally heedless of consequences you'd be dead or in prison already (like anybody else with that combination of traits eventually does). I rather suspect you have too much empathy to easily kill, very little willingness to engage in significant violence, and are able to rationally balance your impulses against serious consequences; since these traits describe the vast majority of people.

Which isn't to say that Hubert is unbelievable, because there are people who are stupid sociopaths.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The emails were actually quite believable in my opinion. Maybe I've just had to deal with an unfortunate amount of awful upper/middle-class sitcom adults, but I could probably list at least ten people who would summon an ancient war deity for something that utterly petty.

And, you know, if I could call down Nergal's wrath on people who use handicapped parking when they have no authorization or need to do so, but use it anyways just because they're lazy assholes… the consequences would probably be worth it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Too obvious, I think…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Does the top image represent Nergal's punch? I can't stop thinking what would happen if this ends in SPC's hands fists…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
…using ancient gods of wrath to solve problems with the neighbors.

That burnout motherfucker across the street who keeps throwing parties better watch the hell out.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So glad I found this again. I completely forgot the sun god's name and after many failed attempts found the article by googling "scp coors light" since the part about him crashing into a tree was indelibly etched in my brain .

Unlike many detractors, I was happy to see the farcical turn at the end. The main body was compelling enough but the ending just sent this thing into orbit for me. I disagree it would've been better played straight.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Why didn't I upvote this before?????? Done now.

Me, I like it. Yes, it's a bit hard on suspension of disbelief, for several reasons. Yes, rich people can be stupid. Yes, sometimes a vendetta with a neighbour can take incomprehensible direction and strength, with no one outside likely to understand why. Yes, sometimes someone can get a completely absurd and stupid idea and just go on with it, day after day after day, with the little voice in their head saying 'don't do this' completely ignored, day after day after day.
I actually like the unbelievability of this story most. Impossible summoning not even the Foundation can reproduce, but successfully done by a rich half-wit with a grudge. IMO, it's done so well that we just have to take it for granted in the end. The mystery of good writing!
Besides…. it's great fun, even though it's not a -J!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This was really disappointing. Granted, I've been putting off reading it since it's release, and before sitting down I didn't realize that the top image was of a giant fist. Had I recognized that earlier I might have been better prepared, but… It lost me as soon as I realized, and from then-on it didn't get me back.

I can say that I liked the first image prior to realizing how stupid it is (in a kind of weirdly awesome way) but the other two are too stock. The description is good before getting into details of documents, and the volume descriptions really flesh out a believable ancient mythology, at the expense of coming across un-Foundation-ly throughout.

Then the emails are just stupid. Obviously that's the point, but it doesn't change the fact that they are. I'd call it unbelievable based on the way it's presented, in addition to pulling any potential believability kicking and screaming out with it. This could just be what I went in with, or forgetting that it was by djkaktus, but I have to downvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This appeared on my Tumblr dash minutes ago and it immediately reminded me of 'the SCP with the big Nergal fist in it'. Not sure if my recent visits to the site and enjoying the new skips have wormed into my brain.

Also, +1 from me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Instead, the guy successfully reads through a 400-page incantation without pause, nukes Dave triumphantly, drinks a pack of beer in celebration and accidentally DWI's himself into a tree.

This is probably the best summation of anything I've ever written.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
As someone who likes this one, I see your point… However, I think this petty-harnessing-of-great-power one works a little better than similar ones because you EXPECT the perpetrator to suffer some sort of great, stereotypical moral consequence for his actions while still on this mortal plane. Y'know? Typically the usual story like this would have ended with Jerry failing the incantation and getting nuked, or Jerry getting nuked by the radius of the same detonation that did Dave in, or getting molested by an eldritch horror or some such.

Instead, the guy successfully reads through a 400-page incantation without pause, nukes Dave triumphantly, drinks a pack of beer in celebration and accidentally DWI's himself into a tree.

Feels like there's something subtly brilliant about that, especially with 90% of the article being an extensive, intricate mythological buildup. Like the elegant punchline this sort of story SHOULD be. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is beautiful. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The details of the ritual were enough for me to upvote, but there are still some issues I had. Even ignoring the fact that some random sitcom dad spent $75,000 on the books, the fact that he could pull off a really intricate ritual when the foundation couldn't stretches disbelief too far. I think I would have liked it better if he half-assed the prayer and blew himself up.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I maybe did bad math upthread not sure, but the current yield of 275 PJ is the equivalent of 65 Mt of TNT or nearly the entire US supply of W87 warheads…

item.title: Re: I liked it
item.content:encoded:
If not for the whole eternal consequences thing I would look into this if I were rich… and could confirm it worked.

You would nuke people if you could afford it? Remind me never to vote for you for a national office.

How much money did he spend?

The 25k was a down payment:

i will wire the rest when i receive the package.

item.title: I liked it
item.content:encoded:
I thought it was good, and upvoted. If not for the whole eternal consequences thing I would look into this if I were rich… and could confirm it worked.

One thing though...
Two things really

In the messages, Mr. Hubert requests information pertaining to SCP-2270, and later purchases the entirety of a nearly complete set of SCP-2270-A for $75,000

and

i have wired the 25 thousand dollars to you

How much money did he spend?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm with Rioghail and Roget on this one. The detail of the Description felt weirdly credible, and I was intrigued, but the realisation that it was just the set-up for a grudge overkill joke was quite deflating.

And then there are lots of details that don't really add up:

+ To wit
- To who
  • Others have touched on the implausibility of that much energy being released without wiping out most of Montana, but I'd like to note also that if this arm hung around long enough to show up on a video recording, then people saw it. There is nothing talking about how half the earth's population needs to be amnesticised for the giant coloured streak they saw heading earthward from the sun. Not even of the person who filmed that video.

Based on current records, it is believed that SCP-2270-B has only manifested once: during Incident 2270-Alpha

We have a record of Nergal manifesting to kill Vasilica as well. And presumably it's got to have worked before Vasilica wrote it down, so that he knew it was worth writing down.

rituals and incantations required to persuade Nergal to attack a large, overwhelming enemy force

But the rituals only give you destruction of a 50m radius, so it won't be much good against an overwhelming force - the rest of them will certainly have overwhelmed you by the time you can read 400 pages aloud for a second time.

  • I struggle to imagine someone who is simultaneously: (a) rich enough not to worry about dropping $75k on some books, when there must be cheaper options to kill Dave; (b) petty enough to put in all of the time and effort required to make the ritual work; (c) stupid enough to use his personal email address for all of this; and (d) smart enough to understand the 8 volume book of "extremely intricate and complicated explanations of rituals and incantations" and get the ritual to work (where the Foundation can't). Things like:

there are a lot of steps in these things. do i need to read all of it for it to work?

what does it mean when it says blood sacrifice

don't make any sense to me in that context - why would he be looking for these books in the first place if he didn't understand what they did or how they worked? If he isn't sure how/if they work, putting in all this time on them doesn't fit with the "sociopathic fixation on revenge"-character that is suggested otherwise. If you're dedicating your life to getting this one guy good, why spend time on anything that may not pay off?

i just read the part about eternal consequences

Why doesn't the Foundation have a copy of Volume VI? They recovered Jerry's manuscripts, and he clearly had a copy of that volume.

  • Why does Dave travel 350 miles across Montana to go hunting, when there seem to be more obvious locations nearby?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
:-X

Why can't it just be cool? Why can't an article that starts with being about doing rituals and summoning some scary demon stay big in scale and seriousness (and maybe go even more over the top :-O) instead of bringing it back down to being about some stupid rich guy who hates his neighbor? It's not cool, it's not really that clever, and I don't really care about it. Which sucks, because this started out really interesting :-P

If it's supposed to be about regular people harnessing power they're not meant to for the sake of petty things, it's too similar to SCP-1291 to appeal to me.

I don't think big scary world-ending demon stuff needs to be subverted like this any more… the wiki has done that enough, right? I want to see apocalypses, demon summoning, and forbidden magic reconstructed instead.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: K yeah okay new favourite for Series 3.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fun fact, that first image might be my favorite shop I've ever done for here. That is, it turned out exactly like I wanted it to.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So yeah, I'd like to bring down the divine wrath of a sun god on the asshole who lives next door to me, too. (I'd spare the dog, though, it's innocent.) We actually tried to have someone lay a curse on him once, no lie.

Anyway, I love the smell of good religious SCPs in the morning. You've got an "exactly point", and "the Montana town of Fort Peck" would sound better as "the town of Fort Peck, Montana/MT", but this works all the way through. It's great. :D

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm just gonna say, that first picture is really fucking cool.

And so is the article in general.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Rich people can be stupid too.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I agree with Rioghail. The e-mails killed it for me, because I have a very hard time believing somebody this imbecilic had the disposable income to drop 25k because he's pissed some guy's dog is pooping on his lawn. It's amusing, certainly, but once you get past the interesting buildup of the volumes it becomes painfully trite and boring in the email segments. If there was more going on in the second half of the article I'd probably like it more, something to add to Jerry's paper-thin character. Maybe him and Dave are competing retired wizards with loads of disposable money on account of wizardry, or some other thing more interesting than dog poop.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I quite like this. The detail of the rituals was the bit that really sold it for me; it really sounded like something with a believable occult following. The punchline was really just icing on the cake at that point.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Looks like there might be a small contradiction -

it is believed that SCP-2270-B has only manifested once: during Incident 2270-Alpha7. Both times SCP-2270-B manifested…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really really really like this one right up until the email logs. I think they sort of detract from the tone a little bit, and I'm not sure they work in this context.

That being said, +1 for everything else.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love this.
I have no problem with the characterization of Mr. Hubert. For a number of years, my mom was the bookkeeper for a private subdivision, and I did a stint as a water meter reader there. Now many of these people were nice, wonderful people who just happened to have a lot of money. But some of them…. there are certain high paying jobs which are well suited to people with severe control issues and/or mild psychopathy. People who would be focused and detail oriented enough to execute the ritual correctly, and yet display the unique mindset that leads to suing a neighbor for damages over an alleged backyard flea and tick infestation caused by said neighbor's dog.1 People for whom, after years of living next door to flocks of lawn flamingos, animated musical Christmas light displays, unraked leaves, and unsightly backyard storage sheds, that damn dog would be the final straw. People who would ignore warnings of eternal tormented servitude to a rage god and the potential for global-scale destruction, and see only the power to smite…*eyes narrow*…Dave.

Footnotes
1. Said dog was a regularly groomed toy breed, and said neighbor's yard had no such problem. And yes. This happened. Twice. With different people.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Pffffffffffffffffffffffffffffffffffffffffffffffffffffffffffft.

good catch though

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Based on current records, it is believed that SCP-2270-B has only manifested once: during Incident 2270-Alpha7.

But then…

Both times SCP-2270-B manifested as a massive, vividly colored arm, extending towards the Earth from the Sun.

So has it manifested once, or twice? Did you get this sandboxed? ;P

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It is still 9.6 Mt which is 27 W78 warhead (the primary strategic warhead in current land-based US usage) yields.

ETA: Just checked the post New START status of forces and I had W78 (350 warheads at 350 kt) and W87 (200 warheads at 300 kt) mixed up.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I actually cackled at the third email. The solemnity of the description was appropriate, but it felt more or less like any other skip — which is not a criticism at all. That's what sets up the surprise of this random jerkoff using terrifying divine power for the stupidest possible reason. Whatever happened to a flaming bag of dog poo on the porch, buddy?

Anyway, well fucking done. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This reminds me of playing various Final Fantasy games and using things like Ultima and Knights of the Round to take out a lone Imp.

Thanks for taking the time to give a good description of each volume's contents, as I quite enjoyed that section.

also i liked the original title better

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Definitely better.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Title's been edited. That may have been a bit much.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was really liking this until the end where you had to turn it into a joke, which created some severe mood whiplash… so no vote from me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm kind of let down by the emails. I feel like I've seen the 'extremely petty individual harnesses great power for absurd reasons' narrative too many times and this doesn't go anywhere with it. There's nothing to Jerry Hubert other than his absurd lack of perspective - he reads like a caricature more than a character, with no motivation other than 'hate this guy to an absurd degree for no reason', with the narrative being manipulated in a slightly unbelievable way to ensure this (would you really start telling a shady occult contact over email about Dave's dog urinating on your lawn?). And while that does serve as an ironic counterpoint to the grandiosity of the object, the undermining being done here seems forced and trite, because it doesn't seem like there's anything behind it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Some of my favorite stories are how people, mere mortals who usually wouldn't stick out, harness the power of magic and otherworld arcana for petty and selfish reasons to meet their own ends. They don't understand the full implications, of course, but it won't stop them. The final addendum (of sorts) after the email is full of nice finishing touches on character development.

I question how the incident was covered up though, if it's as powerful as EmberTech mentions above. That sort of thing can be written off as an explosion at a weapons depot? I'd call bullshit.

This also has one of the worst (in an unfunny way) titles I've ever seen for a mainlister, and gives your hand away far too early for the skip.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's quite a by-the-numbers take on rituals summoning gods/demons, but the fact someone pulled off the complex ritual (and needed readings) show the over-the-top-ness of it.

While we have seen kaktus is capable of terse writing, it is good to see his prose writing again.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is fuckin' great. +1

10/10 wud summon agen

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Let it be known that this wonderful skip was almost named "Fuck This Man In Particular"

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
See what happens when you let your dog go on your neighbor's yard? This is what happens.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: And thank YOU for giving me an excuse to try to figure out how much damage this thing would cause (for anyone curious, it's the equivalent of a 96.5 megaton nuclear bomb).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: No no, Thank YOU for creative content and actively expanding our wonderful SCP database:>

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hey, thanks for reading through this, the entirity of #site19 on January 21st, 2016, between midnight and 1:00AM. I really appreciate it!

I hope the rest of you enjoy it as well!
